## From tokens to model input

An LLM does not receive raw words as input. By the time the sequence enters the
transformer stack, each token has already been mapped to a vector representation.
Lecture 9 focuses on what happens between tokenization and generated output.

The starting point is an embedding matrix `E`. A token id selects one row from this
matrix, producing a dense vector for that token. For a sequence of length `N`, the
model therefore begins with a matrix of token embeddings rather than a list of
strings.

## Positional information has to be added explicitly

Self-attention compares tokens in parallel. That is powerful, but it does not by
itself tell the model which token came first.

This is why transformer inputs also include `positional embeddings`. Without them,
sentences such as "the cat chased the dog" and "the dog chased the cat" would
contain the same token identities but lose crucial order information.

The original transformer paper used sinusoidal positional embeddings:

$$
PE_{(pos, 2i)} = \sin\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right),
\qquad
PE_{(pos, 2i+1)} = \cos\left(\frac{pos}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right)
$$

The practical point is more important than the exact formula: the model needs a way
to represent position as well as content.

## Context length is a real system constraint

The sequence length `N` cannot grow without limit. Attention cost grows quickly with
sequence size, so every model has a finite context window.

That matters for real use:

- long prompts may be truncated,
- the model may throw an error when the limit is exceeded,
- and prompt design has to respect the architecture’s memory budget.

This is one reason tokenization and architecture cannot be treated as separate
concerns.

## The model head turns hidden states into next-token probabilities

After the final transformer layer, the model still has contextual hidden states, not
human-readable text. A `language-model head` converts the hidden state for the last
position into logits over the vocabulary, then softmax turns those logits into a
probability distribution.

The architecture can be summarized like this:

1. token embeddings plus positional information enter the transformer,
2. repeated transformer blocks produce contextual hidden states,
3. the final hidden state is projected back to vocabulary space,
4. softmax gives probabilities for the next token.

The legacy lecture notes also emphasize `weight tying`: the same embedding matrix can
be reused both when mapping tokens into vectors and when projecting hidden states
back toward vocabulary scores.

## Output generation is a sampling problem

Once the model produces a probability distribution, it still has to choose an actual
next token. Different decoding rules create noticeably different behaviour.

- `Greedy decoding` always takes the most likely token. This is stable, but often
  repetitive and generic.
- `Random sampling` uses the full distribution, but can produce too many low-quality
  continuations.
- `Top-k sampling` keeps only the `k` most likely candidates, renormalizes, and then
  samples.
- `Temperature` rescales the logits before softmax. Lower temperature sharpens the
  distribution; higher temperature flattens it.

These choices are part of the system behaviour, not just UI polish. The same model
weights can produce very different outputs depending on the decoding rule.

## Encoding and decoding refer to different transformer roles

The word `encoding` is used for building useful contextual representations of the
input sequence. The word `decoding` is used for generating output tokens one step at
a time.

Modern GPT-style LLMs are mostly discussed as decoder-style systems, but the
original transformer was an encoder-decoder architecture designed for sequence
transduction tasks such as translation.

In encoder-decoder models, the decoder can also use `cross-attention`, where the
decoder queries the encoder output while generating the response. That is the main
architectural difference between the encoder and decoder sides.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students what changes when the same transformer weights are paired with
different decoding rules. This helps separate model parameters from inference
strategy.
:::

## What to report

When students explain LLM input and output architecture in this course, they should
report:

- how token embeddings and positional information are combined,
- why context-window limits matter,
- what the model head does,
- how a decoding strategy changes output behaviour,
- and whether the system being discussed is decoder-only or encoder-decoder.
